coredata.dc:title: Aggregation enhanced FRET: A simple but efficient strategy for the ratiometric detection of uranyl ion
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Dan
Zhang, Li-Jie
Liu, Ming-Hui
Du, Fang-Fang
Shen, Ze-Ya
He, Longwei
Wang, Li-Li
coredata.dc:description: Uranium is one of the most important radionuclides but could also cause potential health risks to human beings due to its radioactive and chemical toxicity. It is an urgent task to develop a simple but efficient sensing platform for UO2 2+, the main existing form of uranium in environment. Herein, a rhodamine-functionalized carbon dots (o-CDs-Rho) was synthesized and applied for UO2 2+ sensing through a simple but novel aggregation-enhanced FRET strategy. The weak FRET efficiency (16.2%) of o-CDs-Rho in dispersed solution is significantly enhanced (>77.2%) after UO2 2+ triggered aggregation due to the increased number of rhodamine acceptors around each CDs from dispersed 80 to aggregated 2800. This is the first ratiometric fluorescence sensor with an inverse change of fluorescence intensity at dual emission wavelengths under single-wavelength excitation for UO2 2+. Under optimized experiment conditions, o-CDs-Rho nanosensor shows a low detection limit of 53 nM and excellent selectivity. Meanwhile, the as-prepared nanosensor also shows high reliability and stability. These excellent properties make it successful in detecting uranium content in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation enhanced FRET
Uranyl ion
Ratiometric fluorescence
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Collection of uranium ore leaching stock solution
Detection of UO2 2+ in ore samples and seawater samples
Synthesis and characteristics of the synthesized o-CDs-Rho
The mechanism of ratiometric sensing of UO2 2+ by o-CDs-Rho
Ratiometric sensing of UO2 2+ by o-CDs-Rho

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22174059 ), Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China ( 2022JJ40363 ), the Young Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province ( 2022RC1230 ) and the University Students Innovation Project of Hunan Province ( S202210555278 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and surface structure of o-CDs-Rho. a) TEM image, diameter distribution (the inserted figure in the top right corner), and high-resolution TEM image (the inserted figure in the bottom right corner). b) AFM image (inset: the height profile measured along the line shown and the height distribution). c) XPS full survey of o-NH2-CDs and o-CDs-Rho. d) FT-IR spectrum of o-NH2-CDs and o-CDs-Rho.

Optical properties of o-NH2-CDs, carboxyl rhodamine, and o-CDs-Rho. UV–vis absorption and fluorescence (excitation and emission) spectra of a) o-NH2-CDs, b) carboxyl rhodamine. c) Spectral overlaps of the fluorescence emission spectrum of o-NH2-CDs and the extinction spectrum of carboxyl rhodamine. d) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence (excitation and emission) spectra of o-CDs-Rho.

Optical response to UO2 2+. Typical fluorescence emission spectra of a) o-CDs-Rho (λex = 430 nm), b) o-NH2-CDs and carboxyl rhodamine mixed directly (λex = 430 nm), c) o-NH2-CDs (λex = 430 nm), d) carboxyl rhodamine (λex = 500 nm) upon addition of UO2 2+. The inset photos show the corresponding fluorescence color before and after addition 50 μM UO2 2+ under 365 nm excitation with a UV lamp.

Response mechanism. a) TEM image of o-CDs-Rho upon addition 50 μM UO2 2+. b) Hydrated particle size of o-CDs-Rho upon addition 50 μM UO2 2+. c) Fluorescence lifetime spectrum of o-NH2-CDs, CDs in o-CDs-Rho and CDs in the o-CDs-Rho aggregates after 50 μM UO2 2+-induced aggregation. d) Fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield of o-NH2-CDs, CDs in o-CDs-Rho and CDs in the o-CDs-Rho aggregates after 50 μM UO2 2+-induced aggregation.

Fluorescence sensing property of o-CDs-Rho. a) Fluorescence spectra of o-CDs-Rho as functions of different concentrations of UO2 2+. Insert: The fluorescence color change under 365 nm UV lamp with the gradual addition of UO2 2+ from 0 μM to 100 μM. b) CIE chromaticity diagram form the fluorescence spectra of the o-CDs-Rho with different concentrations of UO2 2+. c) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (F 585/F 495) and UO2 2+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 μM. Fluorescence intensity ratio (F 585/F 495) response of o-CDs-Rho to UO2 2+ and other metal ions. d) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F 585/F 495) response of o-CDs-Rho to UO2 2+ in the presence of other interferent (100 μM) except for chlorophyll A (10 μM) and phycocyanin (10 μM). The sample was pretreated with triethanolamine for Al3+, and the background fluorescence of phycocyanin was subtracted for phycocyanin to calculate the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 585/F 495).

Detection of uranium in ore samples. a) - c) Fluorescence spectra of o-CDs-Rho upon addition of 20 μL ore-leaching solution from three different samples. d) The uranium content detection and recovery data in ore samples.
Detection of UO2 2+ in seawater samples. a) - c) Fluorescence spectra of o-CDs-Rho upon addition of three different seawater samples. d) The UO2 2+ detection and recovery data in seawater samples.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis and application of the o-CDs-Rho nanosensor for fluorescence detection of UO2 2+.

head.ce:title: Aggregation enhanced FRET: A simple but efficient strategy for the ratiometric detection of uranyl ion

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Province Cooperative Innovation Center for Molecular Target New Drug Study, School of Pharmaceutical Science, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Province Cooperative Innovation Center for Molecular Target New Drug Study, School of Pharmaceutical Science, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Province Cooperative Innovation Center for Molecular Target New Drug Study, School of Pharmaceutical Science, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Urology, Zhongda Hospital, Southeast University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Urology, Zhongda Hospital, Southeast University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Urology, Zhongda Hospital, Southeast University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Uranium is one of the most important radionuclides but could also cause potential health risks to human beings due to its radioactive and chemical toxicity. It is an urgent task to develop a simple but efficient sensing platform for UO2 2+, the main existing form of uranium in environment. Herein, a rhodamine-functionalized carbon dots (o-CDs-Rho) was synthesized and applied for UO2 2+ sensing through a simple but novel aggregation-enhanced FRET strategy. The weak FRET efficiency (16.2%) of o-CDs-Rho in dispersed solution is significantly enhanced (>77.2%) after UO2 2+ triggered aggregation due to the increased number of rhodamine acceptors around each CDs from dispersed 80 to aggregated 2800. This is the first ratiometric fluorescence sensor with an inverse change of fluorescence intensity at dual emission wavelengths under single-wavelength excitation for UO2 2+. Under optimized experiment conditions, o-CDs-Rho nanosensor shows a low detection limit of 53 nM and excellent selectivity. Meanwhile, the as-prepared nanosensor also shows high reliability and stability. These excellent properties make it successful in detecting uranium content in real samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A fluorescence ratiometric sensing platform for UO2 2+ was constructed by rhodamine functionalized Carbon dots base on a simple but efficient strategy: aggregation enhanced FRET. It realized the accurate and selective detection of uranium content in ore and seawater samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Uranium, one of the main natural nuclides for producing nuclear power in varieties of areas such as nuclear weapons, nuclear power, and so on, is widespread existed in rocks and seawater [19,41]. The growing demand for nuclear energy results in the continuously increasing use of uranium [38]. A large amount of uranium-containing waste water was discharged into nature which was generated during uranium mining, spent fuel reprocessing and so on. In nature, uranyl ion (UO2 2+) is the most soluble and stable form among all kinds of oxidation states (+2, +3, +4, +5 and +6) of uranium in the environment [15]. UO2 2+ can accumulate in human organs and causes radiation damage to human organs due to the combined persistent radioactivity and chemical toxicity [28]. Considering the widespread consumption of uranium and its toxicity, deveploping of sensing platforms with excellent performance for detecting UO2 2+ is important, especially the practicability in complex sample [35].

Conventional methods for UO2 2+ determination, such as inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry [24], laser-induced kinetic phosphorimetry [2], ion chromatography [30], Raman spectrometry [1] and electrochemical approaches [10] have low detection limits and satisfactory selectivity. However, these techniques often require expensive instruments, time-consuming pretreatment procedure, and strict control of experimental conditions. To break the shackles of traditional methods, several bio/chemical sensors for UO2 2+ have been developed by using various techniques including fluorometry [11,23,29,3,40,6,7] and colorimetry [22,42]. Due to the intrinsic sensitivity, low cost and high spatial resolution, fluorometry-based UO2 2+ sensors have attracted increased attention. However, most of the developed fluorescence sensors employ the change of emission intensity at a single wavelength as sensing signal that responds to UO2 2+. The single wavelength-based signal acquisition may be influenced by environmental and instrumentation variables [9,36]. In contrast, ratiometric fluorescence techniques rely on the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two different emission wavelengths, so they can provide an intrinsic built-in correction to the environment and instrumentation artifacts, thus offering advantages in terms of improved sensitivity and reduced error [12,33,34].

To develop an accurate sensing platform for UO2 2+, several ratiometric fluorescence sensors have been constructed successfully [26,27,4,5], but these developed ratiometric sensors were designed by introducing another target-insensitive fluorescence emission signal unit as a reference to allow the primary signal to be normalized. Zhang et al. synthesized Zn2GeO4 @ZnS:MnNEs nanoparticle for ratiometric detection of UO2 2+ with a broad detection range [39], however, the change of fluorescence at dual emission wavelengths is irrelevant. Ratiometric probes with opposite and simultaneous changes at different wavelengths enable the dual-wavelength ratiometry and possess a better anti-interference capability, lower background noise and higher signal-to-background ratio. It is regrettable that ratiometric fluorescence sensors with two interrelated detection signals exhibiting opposite and simultaneous changes for UO2 2+ were highly desired. Though Deng et al. developed a multi-component system that contains two individual dyes for ratiometric detection of UO2 2+, it needs twice fluorescence measurements with different excitation wavelengths to obtain one combined spectrum [37].

Due to the excellent photophysical properties, low cost, and easy preparation, carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescence sensor has attracted more and more attention [13,17,18,32,44,8]. The aggregation enhanced Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) strategy, first obersved in the conjugated polymer system [20,25], has been extended to other system recently [16] recently. The increased acceptor concentration around donor and the restricted molecular motion are responsible for the significantly enhanced FRET efficiency. Herein, a rhodamine-functionalized CDs (o-CDs-Rho) nanosensor for ratiometric fluorescent detection of UO2 2+ is proposed based on the simple but efficient aggregation enhanced FRET strategy ( Scheme 1). The as-prepared o-CDs-Rho nanosensor shows remarkable detection sensitivity and selectivity for UO2 2+ with a low detection limit of 53 nM. This integrated ratiometric nanosensor under single-wavelength excitation not only simplifies the operation procedures but also avoids data distortion. The excellent analytical performance of o-CDs-Rho endows them with great feasibility in uranium assay in complex samples.

The chemicals used in this work were purchased from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China) and Macklin (Shanghai, China). All analytical reagents were used without further purification. Inorganic salts used in the experiment were obtained from Tansoole (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm−1) was used in all experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded by JEOL JEM-2100 F electron microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were performed on Bruker MultiMode 8-HR. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained by Thermo Scientific Nexsa using 300 W Al Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was recorded by a PerkinElmer SP2 spectrometer following potassium bromide tableting method. Dynamic laser light scattering (DLS) was used to measure the hydrated particle size (Nano-ZS90, Malvern). UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra were obtained on a Shimadzu UV-2600i spectrophotometer and a Hitachi F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. The fluorescence lifetimes were measured with Edinburgh Instrument EPL-375 (picosecond pulsed diode laser at 375 nm wavelength) as the light source. The pH was measured by Ohaus ST 2100 pH meter.

The CDs were synthesized as previously described [14]. Briefly, 300 mg of o-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 30 mL ethanol and stirred until complete dissolution, then the mixture was placed in a 50 mL Teflon reactor and heated at 180 °C in the oven for 12 h to obtain a raw product. After cooling down to room temperature, the raw product was purified via silica gel column chromatography (petroleum ether/ethyl acetate=1:1) and then dried further by rotary evaporator to afford o-NH2-CDs as a dark brown solid.

The mixture of 1,6-dihydroxynaphthalene (2.00 g, 6.39 mmol) and 2-(4-diethylamino-2-hydroxybenzoyl) benzoic acid (1.02 g, 6.39 mmol) were dissolved with trifluoroacetic acid (15 mL) in a flask, and refluxed at 80 °C for 24 h under N2 protection. After removing the solvent, the product was obtained by recrystallization in ethyl acetate solution.

For the synthesis of o-CDs-Rho, the carboxyl rhodamine (10.0 mg, 0.018 mmol) was first mixed homogeneously with EDCI (38.3 mg, 20 mmol) in 10 mL ethanol, then NHS (11.5 mg, 10 mmol) was added and the mixture was stirred for 1 h at room temperature. Subsequently, 15 mg o-NH2-CDs was added into the reaction solution and stirred for another 8 h to ensure the reaction was complete. The raw products were then purified by high-speed centrifugation and washed with ethanol/ultrapure water (1:1) three times to remove the byproducts and unreacted raw materials including EDCI/NHS and carboxyl rhodamine. Finally, the collected products were dried completely in a vacuum oven at 60 °C overnight to obtain purified o-CDs-Rho.

Fluorescence detection of UO2 2+ with o-CDs-Rho was performed in ethanol/disodium hydrogen-phosphate citric acid buffer (1:1) mixed solution. Typically, certain equivalents of UO2 2+ stock solution (5 mM) were added to 1 mL o-CDs-Rho dispersion solution (20 μg/mL) in a 1 cm quartz cuvette by a micro-injector. Due to the addition of a very small volume of UO2 2+ solution, the volume change of the final solution can be ignored. After mixing evenly, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at 25 °C with an excitation wavelength of 430 nm. The calibration curves were established by plotting the fluorescence intensity ratio between 585 nm and 495 nm versus UO2 2+ concentration. The selectivity for UO2 2+ detection was tested using the same procedure with UO2 2+ replaced by another tested interfering ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection of uranium ore leaching stock solution
ce:section.ce:para: The uranium ore leaching stock solution were provided by School of Nuclear Science and Technology, Unviersity of South China and their uranium from primary sandstone were extracted following the common ore column leaching method. UO3 often exists in the form of hydrate in uranium ore and is dissolved in acid to form UO2 2+. For the UO2 2+ leaching, 0.10 g of ore powder was placed in bottle and the UO2 2+ was extracted by 4.5 mL 70% HNO3 and 1.5 mL 37% HCl at room temperature over 24 h according to the literature [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of UO2 2+ in ore samples and seawater samples
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical experiment for ore leaching stock solution, 20 μL diluted ore extracted solutions or seawater samples was added to 1 mL o-CDs-Rho dispersion solution (20 μg/mL). Then, the fluorescence were measured under excitation at 430 nm. The concentration of UO2 2+ was calculated according to the as-established calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characteristics of the synthesized o-CDs-Rho
ce:section.ce:para: The o-CDs-Rho ratiometric nanosensor was synthesized via a three-step procedure, including o-NH2-CDs synthesis, carboxyl rhodamine synthesis, and the final conjugation step. Fluorescent o-NH2-CDs with abundant amino group on the surface were obtained from o-phenylenediamines via hydrothermal synthesis following the literature [14] with a weight yield of 23.5%. The size distribution and morphology were characterized by TEM. Representative TEM image of o-NH2-CDs shows a size distribution from 1.61 to 7.22 nm, and the average diameter was about 4.24 nm (Fig. S1). The o-NH2-CDs had fine crystallinity and the lattice spacing was about 0.21 nm (insert of Fig. S1), which is consistent with the characteristic (102) diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon. Then FT-IR was carried out to further characterize the functional groups of o-NH2-CDs. The peak observed at 3362, 3298 and 1612 cm−1 in FT-IR spectra were ascribed to the stretching vibration and deformation vibration of the amino group (Fig. S2). The nitrogen element of o-NH2-CDs exists as pyrrolic N (400.4 eV), amino N (399.2 eV) and pyridinic N (398.6 eV) from the XPS analysis (Fig. S3). These data indicate the prescence of amino group in o-NH2-CDs.

Carboxyl rhodamine dye was acquired via a straightforward one-step synthesis from two commercially available compounds (Scheme. S1). The product was purified by recrystalling of the crude product in ethyl acetate with a high yield of 76% and then characterized by 1H NMR, 13C NMR as well as ESI-HRMS (Fig. S4 - S6). The experiment data supported that the isolated product was targeted carboxyl rhodamine dye.

To obtain the o-CDs-Rho nanosensor, the final conjugation step between o-NH2-CDs and carboxyl rhodamine was performed through a simple amide condensation reaction between amino and carboxyl group. The crude product was purified by high-speed centrifugation and washed three times to obtain pure o-CDs-Rho nanosensor. To confirm the successful fabrication of the o-CDs-Rho nanosensor, the morphology of the obtained o-CDs-Rho was first characterized using TEM. As shown in Fig. 1a, the size of o-CDs-Rho was distributed in the range from 1.73 to 7.86 nm with an average diameter of 4.83 nm and the plane-plane spacing was about 0.28 nm. The TEM size of o-CDs-Rho has a moderate increase after grafting with rhodamine compared with the bare o-NH2-CDs (4.24 nm). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) further shows that the hydrodynamic size of o-CDs-Rho is 21.1 nm, much larger than the parent o-NH2-CDs with a size of 11.1 nm (Fig. S7). Then, the topographic height of the as-obtained o-CDs-Rho nanosensor was characterized by AFM (Fig. 1b). The height distributed in the range from 2.41 to 6.54 nm, with an average value of 4.17 nm. XPS and FT-IR were performed to characterize the composition and chemical structure of CDs. The full scan XPS spectrum showed that the C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s binding energies of o-CDs-Rho (Fig. 1c) were located at 285.6 eV, 400.0 eV, 533.1 eV and the element contents of C, N, O was 81.75%, 4.39%, 13.85%, respectively. The decreased nitrogen content and increased oxgen content of CDs attribute to the successful modification of rhodamine dye, which has a much lower nitrogen content (2.54%) and a higher oxygen content (17.41%). The high-resolution scan of the C 1 s (Fig. S8a) confirmed the existence of sp2 carbons (284.8 eV, C-C/CC), sp3 carbons (286.3 eV, C-N/C-O), as well as CO (288.8 eV). N 1 s analysis (Fig. S8b) exhibted the presence of three peaks at 399.4 eV (C-N = C), 400.3 eV (N-H) and 401.4 eV(C-N). The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. S8c) shows two peaks at 532.4 eV (C-O) and 533.8 eV (CO). Next, the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1d) revealed the disappearance of amino groups and the appearance of the amide group in which amino and amide group characteristic bands were observed at 3298/3362 cm−1 for o-NH2-CDs and 3670 cm−1 for o-CDs-Rho, respectively. These data demonstrate the successful modification of rhodamine dye on the surface of o-NH2-CDs to form o-CDs-Rho nanosensors.

As the FRET energy donor, the spectroscopic properties of o-NH2-CDs were shown in Fig. 2a. The UV-Vis spectrum shows obvious absorption peaks at 210, 275 and 427 nm in ethanol solution. The absorption peak below 300 nm was caused by the π-π * transition of the carbon-carbon double bond, whereas the absorption peak located at 430 nm was ascribed to the n-π * transition of amino, demonstrating the larger-sized conjugated sp2 structure of CDs. The maximal fluorescence emission wavelength of o-NH2-CDs was located at 550 nm, which was independent of the excitation and showed the strongest emission intensity with the excitation at 430 nm (Fig. S9a). The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was measured to be 8.89% by using rhodamine 6 G in ethanol (QY=95%) as reference under 365 nm excitation (Fig. S10). The optical properties of as-synthesized CDs were in accordance with the results reported in the literature. Carboxyl rhodamine, as the FRET energy acceptor, shows maximal absorption at 555 nm and fluorescence emission at 600 nm (Fig. 2b). The extinction spectrum of rhodamine dye overlaps well with the fluorescence emission of o-NH2-CDs (Fig. 2c), indicating the possibility of FRET from CDs to rhodamine. As shown in Fig. 2d, the as-prepared o-CDs-Rho nanosensor shows dual emission peaks at 495 and 585 nm under 430 nm excitation, corresponding to the emission of CDs and rhodamine, respectively. The maximum emission wavelength of CDs in o-CDs-Rho has a significant blue shift (55 nm) compared with o-NH2-CDs, which should be attributed to the decrease in the amino group content on the surface of CDs. The charge transfer from amino groups to the carbon core would promote the red shift of the emission wavelength [21,31]. In o-CDs-Rho, the amino group of CDs react with carboxyl group to form amide bonds and thus the charge transfer process was inhibited. In an analysis of the emission spectrum of o-CDs-Rho, weak FRET had occurred from CDs to rhodamine. Like o-NH2-CDs, the emission of o-CDs-Rho is also excitation-independent (Fig. S9b). The emission at 585 nm is weak under the excitation at 430 nm, which is ascribed to the low absorption of rhodamine at 430 nm and the excitation energy is mainly derived from the emission of CDs. However, the low FRET efficiency between CDs and rhodamine results in the weak fluorescence emission of rhodamine. Moreover, the as-prepared o-CDs-Rho shows excellent photostability (Fig. S11) and thermostability (Fig. S12).

In order to calculate the number of rhodamine molecules on each CD, the concentration of rhodamine in the o-CDs-Rho nanosesor and the average molar mass of o-CDs-Rho were calculated. The molar extinction coefficient of rhodamine is 1490 L·cm−1·mol−1 at 550 nm, and the whole concentration of rhodamine in o-CDs-Rho was calculated according to the absorbance at 550 nm using Beer-Lambert law. In order to estimate the average molar mass of o-CDs-Rho, it was viewed as a spheroid, and the average volume of each nanoparticle was calculated according to their topographic heights (∼4.17 nm) and diameter (∼4.83 nm) by the following equation:
(1)
V
1
=
4 π abc
3
The average volume of each nanoparticle was 50.91 nm3, where the value of a, b and c was 2.415, 2.415, and 2.085 nm respectively.

The lattice is cuboid-shaped and the volume of each lattice is calculated according to their plane-plane spacing (∼0.28 nm) and in-plane lattice spacing (∼0.21 nm) by the following equation:
(2)
V
2
= abc

The volume of each lattice is 0.0123 nm3, wherein the value of a, b and c was 0.21, 0.21, and 0.28 nm respectively. The approximate number of lattice is 4139 for each nanoparticle according to the volume of the nanoparticle and each lattice. The average molar mass of o-CDs-Rho is about 49.669 kDa if other elements are regarded as C element. Finally, the computed result revealed that the average number of rhodamine on each o-CDs-Rho nanoparticle is about 80.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of ratiometric sensing of UO2 2+ by o-CDs-Rho
ce:section.ce:para: FRET-based dye-sensitization efficiency depends upon the number of acceptors (rhodamine) within the FRET radius of each donor (CDs). Though the well-overlapped spectra and close distance between CDs and rhodamine, weak FRET from CDs to rhodamine was observed in o-CDs-Rho due to the limited number of rhodamine on per CDs. The fluorescence of o-CDs-Rho shows a significant decrease at 495 nm and an increase at 585 nm upon the gradual addition of UO2 2+ ( Fig. 3a), which indicates the FRET efficiency increases significantly in the presence of UO2 2+. Under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation, the fluorescence color changes from green-yellow to bright orange (insert of Fig. 3a). Therefore, this ratiometric change of fluorescence emission intensity could be applied for the sensitive detection of UO2 2+. Meanwhile, the UV–vis spectra of o-CDs-Rho were unchanged upon the addition of UO2 2+ (Fig. S13), demonstrating that the chemical structure of o-CDs-Rho has not changed. For the control experiment, we mixed o-NH2-CDs and carboxyl rhodamine directly. As shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence intensity around 540 nm and 595 nm were almost invariable with the increasing addition of UO2 2+. In addition, individual o-NH2-CDs or carboxyl rhodamine is also insensitive to UO2 2+ (Fig. 3c, d). Althrough the hydrated particle size of o-NH2-CDs increases dramatically upon adding 50 μM UO2 2+ (Fig. S14), the fluorescence of o-NH2-CDs is invariable, demonstrating that the aggregation is not tight, thereby avoiding fluorescence quenching. Therefore, the covalent link between CDs and rhodamine and incompact aggregation are essential to achieve FRET-based ratiometric sensing of UO2 2+.

In order to investigate the response mechanism, TEM and DLS experiments were performed. TEM result showed that o-CDs-Rho aggregated and formed particles larger than 100 nm in size in the presence of 50 μM UO2 2+ ( Fig. 4a). Similarly, DLS measurements showed a significant increase in the hydrodynamic size of o-CDs-Rho from 21.1 nm (Fig. S7b) to 255 nm (Fig. 4b), about ∼12 times larger in the presence of 50 μM UO2 2+. TEM image and DLS size distribution confirm that the UO2 2+-induced aggregation occurred. Since amino groups are Lewis bases with a strong affinity to hard Lewis acid actinides, the aggregation should originate from the efficient chelation between UO2 2+ and residual amino groups on the surface of o-CDs-Rho.

To further reveal the mechanism of ratiometric fluorescence sensing, the FRET efficiency before and after the addition of UO2 2+ were calculated by the following equation:
(3)
E = 1 −
Φ
DA
Φ
D
= 1 −
τ
DA
τ
D
wherein E represents the FRET efficiency, τ D and τ DA represent the fluorescence lifetime of energy donor before and after FRET, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of o-NH2-CDs, and o-CDs-Rho before and after the addition of 50 μM UO2 2+ were measured. As shown in Fig. 4c and d, the fluorescence lifetime of bare o-NH2-CDs is 4.39 ns and slightly decreases to 3.68 ns after the covalent link to rhodamine. The slight decrease in the fluorescence lifetime of CDs was induced by the low FRET between CDs as energy donors and rhodamine as energy acceptors. Upon the addition of 50 μM UO2 2+ into the solution of o-CDs-Rho, the lifetime further decreased to less than 1 ns. The significant decrease in lifetime indicates a dramatic increase in FRET efficiency. Then the FRET efficiency from o-NH2-CDs to rhodamine was calculated according to Eq. 1 by the decrease of a lifetime. The FRET efficiency is as low as 16.2% in the dispersed state. This low FRET efficiency may be attributed to the limited number of rhodamine molecules around CDs and the less restricted molecular rotation of CDs and rhodamine. An improved FRET efficiency greater than 77.2% from CDs to rhodamine was observed after UO2 2+-induced aggregation of o-CDs-Rho. The number of nanosensors within 10 nm (the effective FRET distance) centered on one o-CDs-Rho was calculated according to the spatial stacking of sphere. Thirty-four nanosensor can be assembled around one o-CDs-Rho after aggregation and the number of effective rhodamine acceptors increases from 80 to 2800 for each CDs energy donor. UO2 2+-caused aggregation induces the increase of local concentration of rhodamine and the energy transfer between CDs and rhodamine is more efficient than the dispersive state, and thus orange emission dominates the solution fluorescence. The aggregation induced green-to-orange fluorescence change was subsequently implemented for UO2 2+ sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric sensing of UO2 2+ by o-CDs-Rho
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the analytical performance of the o-CDs-Rho nanosensor, the detection parameters, including pH and probe concentration were carefully studied. Considering the main existence form of UO2 2+ at pH > 4.0 is the insoluble UO2(OH)2 and UO2(OH)3, the UO2 2+ was dissolved in an acidic aqueous solution (pH = 4.0). The o-CDs-Rho nanosensor is pH-insensitive and the fluorescence is stable in the pH range from 3.0 to 7.0 (Fig. S15). The response of o-CDs-Rho upon UO2 2+ addition at different pH was further measured and the results were shown in Fig. S16. In the presence of UO2 2+, a remarkable increase in fluorescence intensity ratio (F 585/F 495) was observed at all pH (Fig. S17). However, the increment is much weaker at pH < 5, which may attribute to the protonation of the amino group, resulting in the decreased coordination ability of amino group to UO2 2+. The pH of 6.0 with the highest fluorescence ratio, was selected as the experiment condition for UO2 2+ sensing. Since the concentration of o-CDs-Rho may influence their aggregation state, the optimal concentration was optimized. As shown in Fig. S18 and S19, the optimal concentration of o-CDs-Rho is 20 μg/mL.

Under the optimized conditions, the detection of UO2 2+ was performed. Fig. 5a shows the fluorescent spectra of o-CDs-Rho in the presence of variable concentrations of UO2 2+. With the addition of UO2 2+, the fluorescence emission of CDs at 495 nm decreased gradually along with the increase at 585 nm, corresponding to the fluorescence emission of rhodamine. This ratiometric fluorescence emission provided an internal reference for built-in correction, which could reduce environmental interference in the determination of UO2 2+. The CIE titration curve of UO2 2+ further displayed that the FL color changes from green to orange (Fig. 5b). A good linear relationship (R2 > 0.99) was observed for the ratio of fluorescence intensity (F 585/F 495) and the concentrations of UO2 2+ range from 1.0 to 100.0 μM (Fig. 5c), deriving a detection limit of 53 nM based on the following equation: LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation for the blank solution of twenty measurements and k is the slope of the calibration curve. The LOD of the as-established ratiometric method is noticeably below the Environmental Protection Agency contamination limit for UO2 2+ in drinking water of 130 nM. More importantly, the time required for our sensor to respond to UO2 2+ was less than 10 s (Fig. S20), notably a fast response. Compared with other reported methods (Table S1), our sensor displays fast response, high stability and comparable performance of linearity range and LOD. Though the detection limit was not as low as some method reported before, our method provided a wider range of fluorescent color variations (from green to orange), high stability (covalent linkage), low toxicity (avoided the use of highly toxic semiconductor-type quantum dots), and fast response (<1 min). Selectivity is a crucial issue for exploring the potential applications of sensors in a real sample. To evaluate the selectivity of the o-CDs-Rho nanosensor, the effects of other possible coexisting interferents, such as cations (Al3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Na+, Ag+, K+), anions (Br-, Cl-, HCO3 -, SO4 2-), biomolecules (Ser, Gly, Tyr, Trp, VB1, Glucose, Urea, Lecithin) and photoluminescence molecules (chlorophyll A and phycocyanin) on fluorescence intensity were tested. Because the fluorescence emission spectrum of phycocyanin overlaps with that of o-CDs-Rho, the background fluorescence of phycocyanin was subtracted to calculate the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 585/F 495). After data processing, the ratio value was uninfluenced in the prescence of phycocyanin (Fig. S21). By adding different concentrations of these interfering ions to 20 μg/mL o-CDs-Rho solution, no significant change in the ratio of fluorescence intensity (F 585/F 495) was observed except Al3+. However, the influence of Al3+ can be easily eliminated by a simple pretreatment with triethanolamine (Fig. S22). Moreover, the interfering metal ions co-existed with UO2 2+ also had negligible impact on the sensing of UO2 2+ (Fig. 5d). The selectivity of o-CDs-Rho nanosensor can be attributed to the amino and amide groups on the o-CDs-Rho surface, which have high binding affinity for UO2 2+, thereby facilitating the aggregation and resulting in the aggregation-enhanced FRET. These data indicated that this proposed ratiometric fluorescence platform has high selectivity and anti-interference ability for UO2 2+ detection. The excellent sensitivity and selectivity of o-CDs-Rho offer the possibility to detect trace UO2 2+ in real samples quantitatively.

Ionic strength is a crucial factor in the detection of real samples. In order to verify the feasibility in practical application of o-CDs-Rho, the influence of ionic strength was investigated at first. As shown in Fig. S23, ionic strength shows negligible influence on the initial fluorescence ratio even at the concentration of 500 mM, and the nanosensor works well with the gradual addition of UO2 2+. Then, the as-constructed nanosensor was used to determine the concentration of UO2 2+ in leaching stock solutions of three uranium ore samples from Hengyang district. As shown in Fig. 6, the uranium content determined by our method are well in accordance with their accurate concentrations determined by sample provider through ICP-AES with < 5% the relative standard deviations (RSD). Moreover, the utilization of ratiometric o-CDs-Rho nanosensor to seawater samples was further validated. As shown in Fig. 7, the recovery obtained by the new o-CDs-Rho nanosensor ranges from 99.4% to 108.0%, showing that the developed o-CDs-Rho nanosensor is also feasible for quantitative detection of UO2 2+ in seawater samples. All these results indicate the high accuracy of our novel ratiometric nanosensor and suggest a great promise of utilizing the novel o-CDs-Rho nanosensor in uranium mining, process control as well as environmental monitoring.

In summary, a ratiometric sensing platform for UO2 2+ was successfully constructed using CDs as energy donors and rhodamine as energy acceptors. This is the first ratiometric fluorescence sensor that exhibits opposite and simultaneous changes at two different fluorescence emission wavelengths upon the addition of UO2 2+. The sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescence sensing of UO2 2+ was realized through a novel aggregation enhanced FRET strategy. The aggregation can significantly increase the number of rhodamine around each CDs. Moreover, untight aggregation restricts the molecular motion, and thus enhances the fluorescence of rhodamine significantly. Under optimized experiment conditions, the as-synthesized o-CDs-Rho nanosensor showed excellent selectivity over other interfering ions with a LOD of 53 nM. Finally, the concentration of uranium in ores and seawater samples were determined successfully. This study paves a new way for developing robust and low-cost nanosensors for monitoring of UO2 2+ in environment.

The growing demand for nuclear energy results in the continuously increasing use of uranium. A large amount of uranium-containing wastewater generated during uranium mining, raw material processing and spent fuel reprocessing is discharged into the surrounding environment. Uranyl ion is the most soluble and stable form of uranium in the environment, which could accumulate in human organs and cause some serious diseases or even death due to their combined persistent radioactivity and chemical toxicity.

Dan Wang: Investigation, Validation, Data curation. Li-Jie Zhang: Investigation, Validation, Data curation. Ming-Hui, Liu: Data curation. Fang-Fang Du: Result interpretation. Ze-Ya Shen: Data curation. Longwei He: Supervision. Li-Li Wang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22174059), Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2022JJ40363), the Young Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province (2022RC1230) and the University Students Innovation Project of Hunan Province (S202210555278).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2023.131497.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
